title: FlipBench: Measuring Directional Reasoning Asymmetry in Large Language Models
description: We introduce FlipBench, a benchmark of 1,200 matched forward-backward reasoning pairs across four domains (propositional logic, arithmetic, relational reasoning, and function computation) that quantifies the systematic asymmetry between LLMs' ability to derive conclusions from premises versus recovering premises from conclusions. All problems are programmatically generated with verified ground-truth answers, enabling contamination-resistant evaluation.
motivation: Current reasoning benchmarks evaluate LLMs in a single direction (forward: premises to conclusion), conflating genuine understanding with pattern matching. The Reversal Curse showed directional asymmetry in factual recall, but no systematic benchmark measures this for reasoning. Understanding the Directional Reasoning Gap (DRG) is critical for diagnosing whether LLMs truly reason or merely pattern-match, and has implications for model selection, training methodology design, and safe deployment in domains requiring bidirectional inference (diagnosis, debugging, root cause analysis).
proposed_approach: Create matched forward-backward problem pairs across four reasoning domains, each with three difficulty levels. Forward variants require deriving conclusions from given premises; backward variants require recovering premises or inputs from given conclusions or outputs. Evaluate 6-8 open-weight models spanning different sizes (7B-70B) and training paradigms (standard instruction tuning vs. reasoning-optimized RL training). Measure the Directional Reasoning Gap (DRG = forward accuracy - backward accuracy) and consistency rate (both directions correct) as primary diagnostic metrics.

related_work.title: The Reversal Curse: LLMs trained on 'A is B' fail to learn 'B is A'
related_work.authors: Berglund, Tong, Kaufmann, Balesni, Stickland, Korbak, Evans
related_work.how_we_differ: Tests factual recall reversal only, not reasoning. Our work extends the directional asymmetry analysis to logical, mathematical, relational, and computational reasoning with matched problem pairs.

related_work.title: LogicBench: Towards Systematic Evaluation of Logical Reasoning Ability of Large Language Models
related_work.authors: Parmar, Patel, Varshney, Nakamura, Luo, Masud, Mitra, Baral
related_work.how_we_differ: Tests 25 inference patterns individually but does not create matched forward-backward pairs to measure directional consistency on the same underlying problem.

related_work.title: Thinking Forward and Backward: Effective Backward Planning with Large Language Models
related_work.how_we_differ: Proposes methods for backward planning; we create a diagnostic benchmark for measuring the forward-backward gap across multiple reasoning domains.

related_work.title: Lost in Benchmarks? Rethinking Large Language Model Benchmarking with Item Response Theory
related_work.how_we_differ: Uses IRT to diagnose item quality in existing benchmarks. We introduce a new diagnostic dimension (directional consistency) that IRT-based analysis does not capture.

related_work.title: Benchmark^2: Systematic Evaluation of LLM Benchmarks
related_work.how_we_differ: Meta-evaluates benchmarks on ranking consistency and discriminability. Our benchmark tests a fundamentally different property: whether models reason equivalently in both directions.

related_work.title: Variation in Verification: Understanding Verification Dynamics in Large Language Models
related_work.how_we_differ: Studies generation-verification asymmetry. We study forward-backward generation asymmetry, a complementary but distinct phenomenon.

related_work.title: ReasonBENCH: Benchmarking the (In)Stability of LLM Reasoning
related_work.how_we_differ: Measures stochastic instability (variance across runs). We measure structural asymmetry (different reasoning direction on the same problem).

hypothesis: LLMs exhibit a systematic Directional Reasoning Gap (DRG) where forward reasoning accuracy significantly exceeds backward reasoning accuracy on matched problem pairs. This gap is (1) present across multiple reasoning domains, (2) amplified by problem complexity, (3) partially reduced by reasoning-focused training (e.g., RL-trained models like DeepSeek-R1), and (4) domain-dependent in magnitude.
success_criteria: A statistically significant DRG (>5 percentage points) is observed in at least 3 out of 4 domains across the majority of evaluated models
The DRG increases with problem difficulty in at least 2 domains
The benchmark reveals meaningful differences between standard instruction-tuned and reasoning-optimized models
Consistency rates (both directions correct) are significantly lower than the minimum of forward and backward accuracy alone
dataset_size: 1200 matched pairs (300 per domain, 100 per difficulty level)
